question: What are generics in Swift? How do where clauses work?
answer:
Generics let you write code that works for any type while keeping full type-safety — no Any boxing.

• Generic functions: `func swap<T>(_ a: inout T, _ b: inout T)`
• Generic types: `struct Stack<Element> { var items: [Element] }`
• Constraints: `<T: Equatable>` requires conformance.
• `where` clauses add multiple/complex constraints, including same-type and protocol-extension scoping.

The compiler specializes generic code per call site (often with @inlinable / -O), keeping performance close to hand-written code.

question: What are opaque types (some) vs existentials (any) in Swift?
answer:
Two ways to refer to a protocol — they look similar but mean very different things:

• `some Protocol` — opaque type. The compiler picks ONE concrete type and hides it. The function always returns the same concrete type at runtime; callers can chain protocol methods without seeing the underlying type. Zero-overhead.

• `any Protocol` — existential. A box (heap when the value is large) holding any conforming type. Two existentials of the same protocol can wrap different concrete types. Has indirection overhead and can't be used as Self / associated type without limits.

Rule of thumb: prefer `some` for return types and parameters where the concrete type is fixed; use `any` only when you genuinely need heterogeneous storage (e.g. Array<any Animal>).

question: What are property wrappers in Swift?
answer:
Property wrappers package storage + behavior into a reusable annotation. The compiler synthesizes the underlying storage and rewrites property access to call wrapper methods. Behind every SwiftUI `@State`, `@Published`, and `@AppStorage` is a property wrapper.

A wrapper is a struct/class/enum marked `@propertyWrapper` exposing `wrappedValue` (and optionally `projectedValue` exposed via `$`).

Use cases: persistence (@AppStorage), validation (@Clamped), lazy thread-safe init, dependency injection, debouncing, instrumentation.

question: What are result builders (DSLs) in Swift?
answer:
Result builders (originally function builders) let you write declarative DSLs in Swift. The compiler transforms a series of expressions inside a builder-tagged closure into a single composed value via `buildBlock`, `buildOptional`, `buildEither`, `buildArray`.

SwiftUI's `@ViewBuilder`, RegexBuilder, and SwiftPM's package builder are all built this way.

Benefits: readable, declarative client code; static type checking of every clause; easy authoring of testing/UI/parser DSLs.

question: What are key paths in Swift?
answer:
A key path is a typed reference to a property — `\Type.property`. Key paths give you reflection-like ability with full compile-time type safety.

Uses:
• Generic accessors (sort by, group by, dynamic member subscripts)
• SwiftUI's `Toggle("x", isOn: $store.flag)` style — `WritableKeyPath` lets writes too
• Combine's `.assign(to: \.value, on: object)`
• @dynamicMemberLookup with key paths (used by SwiftUI environment values, MapKit, etc.)

Forms: `KeyPath<Root, Value>` (read-only), `WritableKeyPath` (read/write on value types), `ReferenceWritableKeyPath` (write to reference types).

question: Explain Codable in Swift and how to customize JSON encoding/decoding.
answer:
Codable = Encodable & Decodable. Conforming a type lets the compiler synthesize symmetric (de)serialization to/from any DataFormat (JSON, plist, etc.) via JSONEncoder/JSONDecoder.

Customization options:
• `CodingKeys` enum — rename / drop fields
• `keyDecodingStrategy = .convertFromSnakeCase` — auto camelCase ↔ snake_case
• Custom `init(from:)` and `encode(to:)` — full control
• `@propertyWrapper` for repetitive patterns (e.g. dates, optionals with default)
• `userInfo` on encoder/decoder — pass context (e.g. version)

Gotchas: Date/Data formats default to ISO8601-ish; configure on encoder. Throws on missing keys unless field is optional.
